trajectory.description: Observation: The evaluation succeeded with an EER of 12.59% and accuracy of 87.46%. This is a strong baseline for just 2 epochs of training with WavLM-large. Analysis: The model is learning well, and we have plenty of time budget (7.7 hours left). Iteration 6 took ~9.5 minutes for 2 epochs, so 1 epoch takes ~4.75 minutes. We can scale up the training to 15 epochs (~70 minutes) to allow the model to converge. To prevent overfitting and improve generalization to the out-of-domain test set (which contains unseen codecs and attacks), I will add basic data augmentations (random noise, volume scaling, and cutout) to the training dataset. I will also add a CosineAnnealingLR scheduler and save the best model based on validation EER. Decision: Update `dataset.py` with augmentations, update `train.py` to train for 15 epochs with a scheduler and save `best.pt`, run the training, and submit the evaluation using the best checkpoint.

trajectory.description: Observation: The training script failed with a CUDA Out of Memory error after ~13.7 minutes (likely during epoch 2 validation). Analysis: The validation batch size was 64, and the dev set is sorted by length. The longest audio files in the dev set are grouped at the end, creating a batch of 64 very long sequences (up to 15 seconds), which exceeds the 32GB VRAM limit for WavLM-large. Memory fragmentation over multiple epochs likely contributed to it surviving the first few epochs. Decision: I will reduce the batch size to 16 for both training and validation/testing. To maintain the effective training batch size of 32, I will add gradient accumulation (steps=2). I will also add `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` before validation to reduce fragmentation. Then, I will resume training for 15 epochs and submit the evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous training achieved an EER of 8.15%, but I discovered a critical broadcasting bug in `train.py` where `criterion(logits, labels)` was computing a [B, B] loss matrix because `logits` was [B, 1] and `labels` was [B]. Analysis: Fixing this bug should massively improve training efficiency and model performance. Additionally, I will upgrade the model architecture by replacing mean pooling with Attentive Statistics Pooling (ASP), which is standard for speaker/spoofing tasks. I will also add channel simulation augmentations (lowpass/highpass filters) to better prepare the model for the unseen codecs in the test set. Decision: Update `model.py` with ASP, `dataset.py` with filter augmentations, and `train.py` to fix the loss bug. Train for 15 epochs and submit evaluation.

trajectory.description:
Observation: The previous iteration achieved a new best EER of 6.29% on the test set. However, the validation EER reached exactly 0.0000 early on, indicating that the model is overfitting to the dev set and the current augmentations are not strong enough to fully simulate the unseen codecs in the test set. Furthermore, because dev EER hits 0.0000 multiple times, the `best.pt` checkpoint was just the *first* epoch to reach 0.0000, which might not be the most generalized one.

Analysis: To improve generalization to the out-of-domain test set, I need to add stronger augmentations. I will add Mixup (which is highly effective for audio), simulate mu-law codec quantization (since the test set contains a-law/mu-law), and increase the probability of telephone-band filtering. I will also update the checkpoint saving logic to use validation loss as a tie-breaker when EER is identical (e.g., 0.0000), ensuring we save the most confident model. I will increase the epochs to 20 to allow the model to converge with these heavier augmentations.

Decision: Update `dataset.py` with mu-law and stronger filters, update `train.py` with Mixup and the new tie-breaking checkpoint logic, and train for 20 epochs from scratch. Then submit the evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration with Mixup and 20 epochs resulted in a worse test EER (0.0742) compared to iteration 12 (0.0629). Mixup likely destroys the subtle phase and high-frequency artifacts that are crucial for detecting spoofed audio. Additionally, we only have 1.87 hours left in the time budget. Analysis: I need to revert the Mixup augmentation to restore the model's ability to detect spoofing artifacts. I will keep the other augmentations (filters, mu-law, noise) as they help with codec generalization. To ensure the training completes within the remaining 1.87 hours, I will reduce the number of epochs to 13 (which should take ~1.4 hours based on previous iteration timings). Decision: Update `dataset.py` and `train.py` to remove Mixup, set epochs to 13, run training, and submit the final evaluation.
